entry.title: Export and Import tables in SAP from OS level (Control file)
entry.content:
There may be situations where you need to export and import SAP tables from OS level, this can be achieved using R3trans utility in SAP.

Setp 1 :- Collect all the list of tables to be exported.

Step 2 :- Check enough disk space available in the directory where you are going to perform export.

Step 3 :- Create two control files for R3trans which will be used for export & import.

For example :- create control file named as export.ctl and the control file should contain entries like below

export
client=<client no>
file=export_data.dat
select * from <table_name_1>
select * from <table_name_2>
:
:
select * from <table_name_n>


The below are the calling options for R3trans, you can use the below options based on your requirement

 You can specify the following options for calling R3trans:

  • -c <f1> [<f2>] Conversion
    Copies file <f1> to file <f2> by converting the character set to the local character set. If <f2> is omitted, R3trans copies to trans.dat (see Automatic Character Set Conversion with R3trans).
  • -d DB connect
    Check if connect to database is possible and if the ABAP Dictionary is available.
  • -i <file> direct import from data file without specifying a control file (see Import)
  • -l <file> Output of a table of contents to the log file
  • -n Brief information about new features of R3trans not yet documented
  •  -t Test mode All modifications in the database are rolled back.
  •  -u <int> Unconditional mode This option invalidates the rules of the correction and transport system. Each mode can be concatenated (for example -u 12).
  •  -v Verbose mode
    Writes dots to <stdout> and writes additional details to the log file.
  •  -w <file>
    The transport log is written to this file. The default file is trans.log in the current directory.
  •  -x Check whether connection to database can be made

Example:- R3trans export.ctl -w export_log.log

Step 4:- Once export is completed export_data.dat file will get generated.

Step 5:- Use the same .dat file to perform the import, create the import control file (import.ctl) as below

import
client=<client no>
file=export_data.dat


Step 6:- Call R3trans command with the import control file to start import, the table entries will get imported automatically.

Example:- R3trans import.ctl -w import_log.log

Note:- You can even use R3trans to delete a client from OS level, which will remove all the contents of the table with respect to a single client. For that you need to create a control file as below

delete
client=<client no>
select * from 
 
after creating control file you can issue the command as

 Example:- R3trans delete.ctl -w delete_log.log

entry.title: Back Order Processing - Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP in S/4HANA) version 2023 Part II
entry.content:
Advanced Available to Promise (aATP) 2023 – Part 2 

SAP S/4HANA Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP) is a critical component of the S/4HANA suite that enhances the standard ATP (Available-to-Promise) functionality with more advanced and flexible features. It is designed to meet complex order fulfilment processes and ensure higher accuracy and efficiency in inventory allocation and customer order management.

Backorder Processing (BOP)

What is BOP in S/4HANA

In simple word BOP is nothing but rescheduling of all existing orders with some specific rules with some predefined parameters. BOP was also available in ECC system with less parameters for re-scheduling. In S/4HANA now we have many parameters by which we can reschedule the orders and can allocate, distribute and redistribute the quantities between the sales orders in more efficient way.

We have a scenario where we have committed to fulfilling multiple sales orders based on the quantities and inbound delivery dates outlined in a purchase order. However, the vendor has now informed you of a production delay, and only a partial delivery will be possible.

As a result, you need to update the confirmed delivery dates and quantities for both your sales and inventory orders to reflect these changes. To do all this process in mass BOP will help business in accurate way.

Back-order processing in ECC Vs in S/4HANA

Classical rescheduling selects all requirements for a given material/plant combination.

However, BOP (Backorder Processing) allows for more targeted selection of requirements using various criteria. Additionally, the options for sorting these requirements have been enhanced. Selection and sorting criteria are defined within a "segment." One or more segments are linked to a BOP variant and executed together. The segments linked to a variant can employ one of five different confirmation strategies, which determine the extent to which the requirements should be confirmed.

When multiple segments with different strategies are part of a variant, the confirmation quantities will be higher (and closer to the requested date) based on the priority of the strategy used.

Within aATP, SAP S/4HANA has introduced a new confirmation strategy that categorizes your customers into five different groups:

  • Win: When demand exceeds supply, these customers receive top priority for confirmation and fulfilment.
  • Gain: These customers retain their original confirmations when demand exceeds supply, and additional orders will be confirmed if stock remains after all Win group orders are fulfilled.
  • Redistribute: These customers may lose some of their original commitments to the Win or Gain groups when demand exceeds supply. However, if there is remaining stock after these groups are confirmed, they will be prioritized next.
  • Fill: These customers function similarly to the Redistribute group but won’t receive any additional confirmations.
  • Lose: When demand exceeds supply, these customers will lose their confirmations to higher-priority groups.

BOP set up with Fiori apps -

Let us see how we can setup BOP for sales process in SAP using Fiori apps. Below are the apps which we can use to activate BOP in S/4HANA

  1. Configure BOP segment

This feature enables you to establish sorting guidelines for prioritizing various needs, like sales orders and stock transfers, during a Bill of Process (BOP) execution. You have the flexibility to establish criteria based on factors such as customer importance, delivery deadlines, order categories, Available-to-Promise (ATP) classifications, and custom fields, guaranteeing that the most critical demands are handled at the forefront. This sorting takes place within each individual segment of the BOP.

In the 'Configure BOP Segment' app, you need to maintain the sort attribute and sort sequence. The sort attribute refers to standard parameters such as delivery priority, order type, or document category and so on.

Create segments -

Created DF_BOP_WIN with Selection criteria 1 (High) and Sort attribute with delivery priority and sales document type.

  1. Configure Custom BOP Sorting –

Set Up the BOP Segment Application

This application assists in forming BOP segments by combining like requirements - such as sales orders, stock transfer orders (STOs), and reservations - considering factors like material, plant location, customer, order type, and ATP categories. These segments then allow for focused backorder processing using various BOP strategies such as Win, Gain, Improve, Redistribute, Fill, and Lose. You can also link multiple segments to the same strategy in a specific order.

📌For instance, you might establish one segment for high-priority export orders and another for regular domestic orders, making sure each is handled with the appropriate strategy.

The 'Configure Custom BOP Sorting' app is used to set priorities for specific values. For example, if the sort attribute is set to order type, you must define the priority sequence, such as assigning priority 1 to OR, followed by ZOR, and then YOR.

In the 'Configure BOP Segment' app, you must assign the Sort Sequence you created in the 'Configure Custom BOP Sorting' app.

Users can specify the selection criteria and the sorting sequence for the requirement documents. To the right the specific sorting definition is shown.

 

  1. Configure BOP Variant

As per me BOP variant like a game plan. You get to choose the different BOP strategies you want to use and then match them up with the right BOP segments. Each strategy can have more than one segment linked to it.

When you assign several BOP segments to a single strategy, they will run in the order you set them up. In this part, you will take those BOP segments you set up earlier (in Step 1, where you configured them) and connect them to any of the available strategies, like Win, Gain, Improve, Redistribute, Fill, or even Lose.

The BOP variant acts like a filter, deciding which requirements get included in the process and how they are handled. These set variants are what you will use later on to plan your BOP run. Once everything is set up, the system lets you test out the variant in real-time, and you can even make a copy of it to use with a different strategy.

  1. Schedule BOP Run

Once the BOP variant is created, users can then indicate which variant they'd like to have scheduled. The job can be set to run right away or at a specific time in the future, and users can also set how often it should run. Users have the choice between running a simulation or an actual execution that will update orders.

Users can also turn on the log feature, which creates logs that are then available for viewing within the BOP monitoring application.

 

  1. Monitor BOP Run

Once Job is finised we can Monitor BOP run.

The executed jobs including simulations, can be seen here with details of:

  • Variant name, username, job details and processing status
  • Overall confirmations and details of old confirmation quantity vs. the new confirmation quantity
  • Overall supply assignment quantity and details of old vs. new assignment quantities

📌Let us take live example to check the BOP run result

I created two sales orders, 27 and 28, with quantities of 100 and 50, respectively. Both orders have a customer priority of 2 (Medium).

Currently, there is no stock available, so the quantities remain unconfirmed. To expedite the process, I uploaded 150 units. After running the BOP, the full quantity for both sales orders was successfully confirmed.

After the job is completed, we can track sales order confirmation changes using the 'Monitor BOP Run' app. The system displays both the previous and updated allocations for sales orders, as shown in the example below.

Sales orders 27 and 28 now have fully confirmed quantities, with a delivery priority of 2 (Medium).

Now, let's create a sales order for a different customer with a delivery priority of 1 (High). Upon creation, the system initially confirms zero quantity. The expected behaviour is that the system should redistribute stock from the first two sales orders and allocate it to the new sales order 29 after the BOP run, in line with our WIN strategy.

As shown in the screenshot above, after the BOP run, the system removed the confirmation of 100 units from sales order 27 and 50 units from sales order 28. These released quantities were then allocated to sales order 29, even though it was created later in the system.

Fallback Functionality in SAP Backorder Processing (BOP)

In SAP Advanced ATP (aATP) Backorder Processing (BOP), the fallback functionality ensures that requirements (such as sales orders or stock transfer orders) still receive an allocation if they do not meet the criteria of their initially assigned BOP strategy (Win, Gain, Improve, Redistribute, Fill, Lose).

Example Scenario

- Sales Order A is assigned to the WIN strategy, but due to stock shortages, it cannot be fully confirmed.

- Instead of remaining unconfirmed, the system reassigns it to a fallback strategy like GAIN or REDISTRIBUTE, allowing it to receive at least a partial allocation.

- This helps maintain a balanced and efficient stock allocation process.
